_note: Anna v2 translation set — short B1 prompts, single-target, low typo surface. Replaces the v1 (tr_anna_001-015) which proved too hard at MVP (raw 1/15, ~7 false negatives from typos + article drops). Each prompt is 3-5 Russian words; expected English is 3-6 words; one grammatical target per exercise. Article-stripping in s90r2 normalize() means a/an/the variants don't need to be enumerated in correct_answers. Author-tested mentally for typo surface (avoided neighbour, comfortable, daughter, vocabulary, expectations).

exercises.source_observation: Single-target B1 replacement post Anna's v1 session — wait + for in isolation, no compound clause
correct_answers: I am waiting for a friend
I am waiting for my friend

common_errors.feedback: Russian 'жду' takes no preposition, but English 'wait' requires 'for'. ✗ 'I'm waiting friend' / 'I'm waiting a friend'. ✓ 'I'm waiting for a friend'.
Easy mix-up: 'wait FOR' but 'expect' takes NO preposition. ✗ 'expecting for a friend'. ✓ 'expecting a friend'. (Both 'wait for' and 'expect' work for 'жду' — pick one and don't mix.)

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm waiting for a friend' (or 'expecting a friend'). The verb 'wait' always needs 'for' in English.

exercises.source_observation: Single-target B1 — depend on (Russian 'зависит от' calque trap)

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'depend from' — direct calque of Russian 'зависеть от'. English fixes the preposition: 'depend ON'. Always 'on'.
✗ 'depend of' is wrong. The fixed preposition is 'depend ON' — never 'of', never 'from'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Three-word target: 'It depends on you'. The fixed preposition for 'depend' is always 'on'.

exercises.source_observation: Single-target B1 — listen to (Russian 'слушаю + acc' has no preposition)

common_errors.feedback: Russian 'слушать музыку' has no preposition, but English 'listen' always takes 'to'. ✗ 'listen music'. ✓ 'listen TO music'.
✗ 'listen on' — wrong preposition. The fixed phrase is 'listen TO': 'listen to music', 'listen to the news', 'listen to me'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm listening TO music' (or 'I listen to music'). 'Listen' always needs 'to' before what you're listening to.

exercises.source_observation: Single-target B1 — look for (vs look at)
correct_answers: I am looking for the keys

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'look at' = look in someone's direction (visual focus). For 'ищу' (search/seek) use 'look FOR': 'I'm looking for keys' = trying to find them.
✗ 'search keys' — English 'search' takes 'for' when you mean find: 'searching for keys'. (You can also say 'looking for keys'.)

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm looking for keys' (or 'searching for keys'). The 'for' is required — without it, 'look' means visual focus, not search.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'think on' — wrong preposition. Use 'think ABOUT' or 'think OF' for someone/something.
✗ 'think for' — wrong. Russian 'думать о' maps to English 'think ABOUT' or 'think OF', never 'think for'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm thinking about you' or 'I'm thinking of you' — both correct. Russian 'о' here = 'about' or 'of'.

exercises.source_observation: Single-target B1 — 'on Saturday' (Russian 'в субботу' uses one preposition for everything)
correct_answers: We'll see each other on Saturday

common_errors.feedback: Days take 'on', not 'in': 'on Saturday', 'on Monday'. ✗ 'in Saturday'. Russian 'в субботу' has one preposition; English splits — 'on' for days, 'in' for months/years, 'at' for clock times.
✗ 'at Saturday' — 'at' is for clock times (at 7), not days. Days take 'on': 'on Saturday'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'See you ON Saturday'. Days of the week always take 'on' in English.

common_errors.feedback: Months take 'in', not 'on' or 'at': 'in April', 'in July'. ✗ 'on April'. Russian 'в апреле' uses one preposition; English splits by time unit.
Missing 'in' — months always need a preposition: 'born IN April', not 'born April'. The 'in' is mandatory.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I was born IN April'. Months always take 'in' in English.

exercises.source_observation: Single-target B1 — make a decision (vs Russian 'принять решение' calque to 'take')

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'take a decision' — direct calque of Russian 'принять решение'. The standard English collocation is 'MAKE a decision'.
✗ 'do a decision' — wrong delexical verb. Use 'MAKE'. Mnemonic: you MAKE choices and decisions; you DO tasks and chores.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Three-word target: 'Make a decision'. In English the verb is 'MAKE' (not 'take', not 'do').

exercises.source_observation: Single-target B1 — do homework (vs Russian 'делать уроки' calque to 'make')

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'making homework' — Russian 'делать уроки' calques to 'making' but English uses 'DO': 'doing homework'. MAKE for things you create; DO for tasks/chores.
✗ 'homeworks' — 'homework' is uncountable in English, no plural -s. Russian 'уроки' looks plural but 'homework' stays singular.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm DOING my homework' (uncountable — no plural -s).

exercises.source_observation: Single-target B1 — cook/make dinner (both valid; tests delexical 'do' anti-pattern + dropped auxiliary)

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'doing dinner' — for meals use 'cook' or 'make', not 'do'. ✓ 'cooking dinner' / 'making dinner'.
Missing auxiliary 'am' — present continuous needs BE + verb-ing: 'I am cooking' or 'I'm cooking', never just 'I cooking'. (Or use simple present: 'I cook dinner' — also fine.)

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm cooking dinner' or 'I'm making dinner' (both correct). Don't say 'I cooking' — present continuous needs 'am'.
